anchor.snippet: a stationary point of a differentiable function of one variable is a point on the graph of the function where the function's derivative is zero
a stationary point is a point on the surface of the graph where all its partial derivatives are zero
they correspond to the points on the graph where the tangent is horizontal
A turning point of a differentiable function is a point at which the derivative has an isolated zero and changes sign at the point
A turning point is thus a stationary point, but not all stationary points are turning points

annotations.label: Non-turning stationary points are horizontal inflection points

anchor.snippet: If the function is twice differentiable, the isolated stationary points that are not turning points are horizontal inflection points

annotations.label: Inflection point that is not a turning point

anchor.snippet: which is also an inflection point, but is not a turning point
a local minimum ( minimal turning point or relative minimum ) is one where the derivative of the function changes from negative to positive
a local maximum ( maximal turning point or relative maximum ) is one where the derivative of the function changes from positive to negative
a rising point of inflection (or inflexion ) is one where the derivative of the function is positive on both sides of the stationary point
a falling point of inflection (or inflexion ) is one where the derivative of the function is negative on both sides of the stationary point
The first two options are collectively known as
is called a global (or absolute) extremum
The last two options—stationary points that are not local extrema—are known as saddle points
By Fermat's theorem , global extrema must occur
can in some cases be determined by examining the second derivative
A more straightforward way of determining the nature of a stationary point is by examining the function values between the stationary points

are those points x 0 where the derivative in every direction equals zero
this point is not a point of inflection
But this is not a stationary point, rather it is a point of inflection
This is both a stationary point and a point of inflection
The point 0 is a non-isolated stationary point which is not a turning point nor a horizontal point of inflection
0 is a stationary point that is not isolated
